content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 207 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

August 3, 1977

Robert E. Roney
City Attorney
City of Portola
P.O. Box 1225
Portola, California 96122

Dear Mr. Roney:

This is in response to your letter of 21 July 1977, requesting the Commission to render advice concerning a potential conflict of interest under Government Code §87100 and 87103(d). In your case, the mayor of the city proposes to sell an antique vehicle to an engineering firm, Walters Engineering, which has, in the past and may, in the future, contract with the city to provide certain engineering consulting services.

If the mayor sells his vehicles to Walters, the firm will be a "source of income" to him for the twelve month period following such sale. See, §87103; §82010. Accordingly, the mayor may not make or participate in making decisions that foreseeably will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on Walters, Government Code §87100 and 87103. However, as initiated in your letter, and as we discussed on the telephone, both the size and nature of the contract in question, and the size and diversity of Walters' receipt of income to conclude that any effect on Walters will not be "material" within the meaning of §87103.

The Commission, by regulation, has further defined "material financial effect". That regulation provides, in relevant part:

16702. The financial effect of a governmental decision on a financial interest of a public official is material if, at the time the official makes, participates in making or attempts to use his or her official position to influence the making of the decision, in light of all the circumstances and facts known at the time of the decision, the official knows or has reason to know that the existence of the financial interest might interfere with the official's performance of his or her duties in an

Robert F. Roney
August 3, 1977
Page Two

impartial manner free from bias.

(b) In determining the existence of a
material effort upon a financial interest,
consideration should be given, but not be
limited to, an analysis of the following
factors:

...(3) In the case of a source of income of
a public official as defined in Government
Code §27103(c):

(A) The decision will affect the
source of income in the manner prescribed
in subsection (b)(1) above;

(B) Whether the governmental decision
will directly affect the amount of income
to be received by the official;

(C) Whether there is a nexus between
the governmental decision and the purpose
for which the official receives income...

2 Cal. Adm. Code §18702

To the extent that the contract is made personally with
Mr. Eric Beyer, and not with the firm itself, the financial
efforts of the contract on the firm will be at minimum and
clearly will not meet the standards raised in subsection 3(A)
of the Regulation. Furthermore, since Walters is a very large
firm with annual gross revenues in excess of one million dollars,
it does not appear that the regulation criteria would be met
even if the contract were made directly with the firm. Factors
4(B) and 5(C) of the cited Regulation are clearly not present
in the instant situation.

1/ Whether the effect of the decision will be to increase or
decrease the annual gross income or revenues of the business entity
by one percent or more or the annual net income of the business
entity by .5 percent or more;

Whether the effect of the decision will be to increase or
decrease the assets or liabilities of the business entity
by $50,000 or more or by .5 percent of its current assets
or liabilities, whichever is less.

2 Cal. Adm. Code §18702b(1)(A) & (B)

. Robert F. Roney
August 3, 1977
Page Three

I would further point out that in terms of the general rule
of the cited regulation, i.e., whether "the existence of the
financial interest might interfere with the official's performance
of his or her duties in an impartial manner free from bias," the
following considerations are also relevant. It appears that the
transaction (said) between Walters and the mayor is a "one-shot
deal"; that fair market value was paid for the vehicle; and that
there is no expectation of any continuing relationship or flow
of income between the parties.

Taking all these factors into consideration, it does not
appear that the disclosures concerning the conflict of interest have a
"material financial effect" on Walters. To the extent the facts
are as indicated, supra, the mayor may participate in those
decisions without being in violation of Government Code §87100.
I trust this answers your question.

Thank you for writing and for your interest in the Political
Reform Act. If I can be of further assistance in this or any
other matter, please feel free to call on me at (916)322-6444.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Kenneth W. Goshorn
Kenneth W. Goshorn
Research Specialist III
Conflicts of Interest Division

KWG:mfa

City of Portola
P.O. BOX 1225
PORTOLA, CALIFORNIA 96122
Telephone (916) 832-4217


July 21, 1977


FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Attention: TIM GOSHORN

Gentlemen:

The undersigned is Portola City Attorney, and writes in
that capacity.

This letter follows a telephone call to (Tim Goshorn)
the FPPC requesting advice re. possible conflict of interest
under Government Code Section 87103, sub. c.

FACTS: The Mayor of the City of Portola, a general law city,
owns a 1930 Ford "A" pickup truck, fully restored, and of the
approximate market value of $3,500.00. The Mayor also owns a
similar antique Ford coupe, and has considered sale of the
pickup; he has been asked to sell to Walters Engineering, an
incorporated consulting engineering firm in Reno, Nevada.
Walters wants to use the antique pickup for business promotional
purposes.

Walters Engineering has done some consulting work for the
City in the past, the last services being finished about two
years ago. The City is in the process of employing a consulting
engineer for work in connection with a municipal water improvement project; this engineer may be associated with Walters
Engineering if certain engineering work is required. The total
value (cost) of the consulting services would probably be only
about $5,000.00; Walters Engineering has been engaged in con-
sulting work, primarly involving water and sewer projects for
public agencies in Nevada and California + 10 years and has a
substantial number of clients.

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
July 21, 1977

PAGE TWO


QUESTION: On these facts, would a cash sale of the vehicle
to Walters Engineering for approximately $3,500.00 (assumed
to be in fact market value) be "material" within the meaning
of Section 87103(c)?

Sincerely,

[illegible signature]

ROBERT E. RONEY
City Attorney

RER:br

sections.question: On these facts, would a cash sale of the vehicle to Walters Engineering for approximately $3,500.00 (assumed to be in fact market value) be "material" within the meaning of Section 87103(c)?
sections.facts:
The Mayor of the City of Portola, a general law city, owns a 1930 Ford "A" pickup truck, fully restored, and of the approximate market value of $3,500.00. The Mayor also owns a similar antique Ford coupe, and has considered sale of the pickup; he has been asked to sell to Walters Engineering, an incorporated consulting engineering firm in Reno, Nevada. Walters wants to use the antique pickup for business promotional purposes.

Walters Engineering has done some consulting work for the City in the past, the last services being finished about two years ago. The City is in the process of employing a consulting engineer for work in connection with a municipal water improvement project; this engineer may be associated with Walters Engineering if certain engineering work is required. The total value (cost) of the consulting services would probably be only about $5,000.00; Walters Engineering has been engaged in consulting work, primarily involving water and sewer projects for public agencies in Nevada and California + 10 years and has a substantial number of clients.

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION  
July 21, 1977

PAGE TWO

In your case, the mayor of the city proposes to sell an antique vehicle to an engineering firm, Walter's Engineering, which has, in the past, and may, in the future, contract with the city to provide certain engineering consulting services. Accordingly, the mayor may not make or participate in making any decision that foreseeably will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on Walters.
